title: Bandwidth Planner Guide
summary: Understand active device demand, per-stream bandwidth, peak factor, protocol overhead, other traffic, uplink capacity, and utilization target.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates aggregate network demand from active streams or devices, adds peak and overhead assumptions, and compares the result against the current uplink capacity.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch network pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning camera traffic, endpoint groups, branch uplinks, network closets, aggregation links, or any design where total traffic demand needs to be checked before the uplink becomes the limiter.
bullets: Early network design planning.
Expansion, migration, or upgrade review.
Existing-system troubleshooting or validation.
Explaining network tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical network planning terms.

items.label: Active streams / devices
items.description: The number of concurrent devices, users, cameras, streams, or traffic sources included in the estimate.
items.whyItMatters: This is the multiplier for the traffic model. More active sources increase both average demand and peak demand.
items.commonMistake: Counting installed devices instead of the number expected to be active at the same time.

links.label: Check oversubscription from this load
links.note: Use this when the bandwidth estimate feeds an access, aggregation, or WAN capacity check.

items.label: Average Mbps per stream / device
items.description: The typical sustained bandwidth consumed by each active stream or device.
items.whyItMatters: The per-device value controls the base traffic load before peak factor and overhead are added.
items.commonMistake: Using a best-case codec or idle value instead of a realistic active value.

items.label: Peak factor
items.description: A multiplier that converts average demand into a more realistic peak-planning load.
items.whyItMatters: Networks are usually stressed by peaks, not averages. Peak factor keeps the design from being sized too tightly.
items.commonMistake: Leaving peak factor at 1.0 when traffic has obvious busy periods.

links.label: Project peak growth over time
links.note: Use this when the peak multiplier also needs to be projected into future months.

items.label: Protocol overhead (%)
items.description: A percentage allowance for headers, framing, transport overhead, retransmits, management traffic, and similar non-payload traffic.
items.whyItMatters: Overhead reduces the usable portion of the uplink for the actual application payload.
items.commonMistake: Assuming payload Mbps and link Mbps are the same thing.

links.label: Account for VPN overhead
links.note: Use this when tunnels or encryption are part of the path.

items.label: Other traffic allowance (Mbps)
items.description: Extra bandwidth reserved for traffic not modeled by the stream/device count.
items.whyItMatters: Unmodeled traffic still consumes the same uplink and can push an otherwise clean design into congestion.
items.commonMistake: Modeling only the headline workload and forgetting everything else on the link.

items.label: Current uplink capacity (Mbps)
items.description: The usable bandwidth of the uplink being checked against the modeled demand.
items.whyItMatters: This is the capacity boundary for the planner. A high demand result is only useful when compared with the actual link being used.
items.commonMistake: Entering switch port speed when the real bottleneck is a smaller ISP, firewall, or upstream path.

links.label: Compare access and WAN layers
links.note: Use this when the same load crosses multiple network layers.

items.label: Target max utilization (%)
items.description: The preferred upper operating band for the uplink after demand, peaks, overhead, and other traffic are included.
items.whyItMatters: A lower target leaves more space for bursts, retransmits, failover, and growth.
items.commonMistake: Treating 100% line rate as a safe operating target.

links.label: Simulate when utilization runs out
links.note: Use this when you need to know how long the link stays inside the target band.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means modeled peak demand is comfortably below the target utilization band. Watch means the uplink may work but has limited reserve. Risk means traffic demand is likely too close to, or above, practical uplink capacity.
bullets: Healthy: the entered assumptions leave practical operating margin.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumption deserves review before relying on it.
Risk: reduce demand, increase capacity, change the topology, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most network planning mistakes come from checking one layer or one average value while ignoring peaks, overhead, path behavior, failure conditions, and growth.
bullets: Planning from theoretical line rate instead of usable sustained throughput.
Sizing for calm-period average traffic while ignoring peaks, bursts, retransmits, and maintenance activity.
Treating one network layer in isolation even though access, aggregation, WAN, VPN, and endpoint behavior interact.
Forgetting to document assumptions before the design becomes hardware, cabling, or service-provider work.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, firewall/router/switch limits, service-provider terms, cabling validation, monitoring data, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Real measurements from the installed network are better than estimates whenever they are available.
Protocol overhead, encapsulation, device behavior, cabling, switching, routing, and WAN conditions can change the result.
Healthy results still need validation against the actual switch, firewall, router, ISP, tunnel, or endpoint platform being used.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, vendor validation, production testing, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, firmware, service-provider limits, and network architecture being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for performance, outage behavior, security, and operational recovery.
Document assumptions before implementation and revisit them as traffic or topology changes.
